expanded_description: The equation of a line expresses the relationship between x and y for every point on the line. Two primary forms—slope-intercept (y = mx + b) and point-slope (y − y₁ = m(x − x₁))—allow lines to be described algebraically from their slope and a point. These forms are essential tools for graphing lines, finding intersections, modeling linear relationships, and connecting the geometric properties of lines to their algebraic representations.

core_concept.principle: A linear equation in two variables represents a straight line in the coordinate plane. The slope-intercept form y = mx + b explicitly shows the slope m and the y-intercept b. The point-slope form y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) uses any known point (x₁, y₁) and the slope m. Both forms describe the same line and can be converted into each other, as well as into standard form Ax + By = C.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a line's equation as its 'fingerprint'—a unique algebraic description that identifies every point on the line. The slope-intercept form is like reading a person's height (slope) and where they stand on the y-axis (y-intercept). The point-slope form is like knowing their height and one specific location they've been. Both give you enough information to find them anywhere. These forms are the bridge between the visual geometry of lines and the algebraic world of equations—once you have the equation, you can find any point, predict intersections, and analyze relationships without ever drawing the line.

image.alt: A coordinate plane showing a line. Two equation forms displayed: y = 2x + 3 (slope-intercept: m=2, b=3) and y − 5 = 2(x − 1) (point-slope: m=2, point (1,5)). The y-intercept (0,3) and the point (1,5) are plotted on the line. The slope triangle showing rise=2, run=1 is visible.
image.caption: Slope-intercept form (y = mx + b) and point-slope form (y − y₁ = m(x − x₁)) — two ways to write the same line.

sections.introduction: The slope-intercept form is the most commonly used form of a linear equation. It displays the slope and y-intercept directly, making it easy to graph a line or to identify its key features at a glance. Every non-vertical line can be written uniquely in this form.

introduction_image.alt: A line graphed on a coordinate plane. The y-intercept is labeled (0, b) where b = 3. The slope triangle shows m = 2/1 = 2. The equation y = 2x + 3 is displayed with m = 2 and b = 3 highlighted. Arrows connect m to the slope triangle and b to the y-intercept.
introduction_image.caption: Slope-intercept form: y = mx + b. m = slope, b = y-intercept (where the line crosses the y-axis).

key_concept.definition: The slope-intercept form of a linear equation is y = mx + b, where m is the slope of the line and b is the y-intercept—the y-coordinate of the point where the line crosses the y-axis (0, b). Every non-vertical line has a unique slope-intercept equation.
key_concept.context: This form is ideal for graphing (plot the y-intercept, then use slope to find more points), for identifying slope and y-intercept at a glance, and for writing equations when the slope and y-intercept are known. It is the default form for most applications.

image.alt: A line with equation y = −3/2 x + 4. The y-intercept (0,4) is circled. The slope m = −3/2 is illustrated with a slope triangle: from the y-intercept, go down 3 and right 2 to reach another point (2,1). The parts of the equation are labeled: y = m x + b.
image.caption: y = mx + b: m tells the direction and steepness; b tells where the line starts on the y-axis.

process_flow.description: Determine the slope of the line and the y-coordinate where the line crosses the y-axis.
process_flow.mechanism: The slope m can be given, calculated from two points using the slope formula, or determined from parallel/perpendicular information. The y-intercept b is the y-value when x = 0—either given directly or found by substituting a known point into the equation after the slope is known.
process_flow.result: Two numbers: m (the slope) and b (the y-intercept).
applications: Given slope m = 4 and y-intercept b = −2 → equation: y = 4x − 2.
Given two points: find m first using slope formula, then find b by substituting one point.
Given a graph: read b where the line crosses the y-axis, count rise/run for m.

image.alt: A coordinate plane showing a line. The y-intercept is circled at (0, −2). A slope triangle from (0,−2) to (2, 6) shows rise=8, run=2, so m=4. Labels: 'm = 4' and 'b = −2'. The equation y = 4x − 2 is ready to be written.
image.caption: Step 1: Find the slope m and y-intercept b from the graph or given information.

process_flow.description: Write the equation by placing the slope in the m position and the y-intercept in the b position.
process_flow.mechanism: Replace m with the slope value (keep the sign) and b with the y-intercept value. The equation is y = mx + b. No parentheses are needed around m or b. If m is a fraction, it can be written as a coefficient of x. If m is 1, write simply x (not 1x). If m is −1, write −x.
process_flow.result: The complete slope-intercept equation of the line.
applications: m = −4, b = 0 → y = −4x (the line passes through the origin).

image.alt: The template y = mx + b with arrows showing m=2/3 and b=5 being placed into the formula. Result: y = (2/3)x + 5. The line is graphed with the y-intercept at (0,5) and slope triangle showing rise=2, run=3.
image.caption: Step 2: Substitute m and b into y = mx + b.

process_flow.description: Plot the y-intercept, then use the slope to find additional points and draw the line.
process_flow.mechanism: Plot the point (0, b). From this point, use the slope as rise/run: move vertically by the rise and horizontally by the run to plot a second point. Connect the points with a straight line extending in both directions. For negative slopes, make one of rise or run negative (typically, keep run positive and make rise negative for a falling line).
process_flow.result: An accurate graph of the line using only its equation.
applications: y = 2x + 1: Plot (0,1). Slope 2 = 2/1: from (0,1), go up 2, right 1 to (1,3). Draw line.
y = −3/4 x + 2: Plot (0,2). Slope −3/4: from (0,2), go down 3, right 4 to (4,−1). Draw line.
y = −x: Plot (0,0). Slope −1: from (0,0), go down 1, right 1 to (1,−1). Draw line.

image.alt: A line being graphed step by step: (1) y-intercept (0,1) plotted. (2) From (0,1), slope 2 takes you to (1,3). (3) These two points connected with a straight line extended with arrows. The equation y = 2x + 1 is displayed.
image.caption: Step 3: Graph the line — plot b on the y-axis, then use rise/run to find more points.

classifications.value: Horizontal and vertical lines, and lines through the origin, have simplified equations.
characteristics: Horizontal line: m = 0 → y = 0x + b → y = b. All points have the same y-coordinate.
Vertical line: slope undefined → cannot be written in slope-intercept form. Equation: x = a.
Line through origin: b = 0 → y = mx. The y-intercept is at (0,0).
classifications.behavior: These special cases are common and should be recognized instantly. A horizontal line has equation y = constant. A vertical line has equation x = constant. A line through the origin has no constant term.

examples.explanation:
Horizontal line through (0, 5): m = 0, b = 5 → y = 5.

Vertical line through (3, 0): undefined slope → x = 3 (cannot use y = mx + b).

Line through origin with slope −2: y = −2x.

image.alt: Three lines: (1) Horizontal y=5. (2) Vertical x=3. (3) Line through origin y=−2x. Each with equation and key features labeled.
image.caption: Special cases: horizontal (y = constant), vertical (x = constant), through origin (y = mx).

image.alt: Complete section summary: the slope-intercept formula, the three-step process for writing and graphing, special cases, and examples with various slopes and intercepts.
image.caption: Slope-Intercept Form overview: formula, process, and special cases.

sections.section_title: Point-Slope Form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁)
sections.introduction: Point-slope form is the most flexible way to write a linear equation. It requires only the slope and any one point on the line—not necessarily the y-intercept. This makes it ideal when the y-intercept is unknown or inconvenient to find.

introduction_image.alt: A line passing through point (3, 5) with slope 2. The point-slope form is displayed: y − 5 = 2(x − 3). The point (3,5) is highlighted on the line. The slope triangle shows m=2. An arrow shows that expanding gives y = 2x − 1 (slope-intercept form).
introduction_image.caption: Point-slope form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). Uses any point on the line, not just the y-intercept.

key_concept.definition: The point-slope form of a linear equation is y − y₁ = m(x − x₁), where m is the slope and (x₁, y₁) is any point on the line. This form directly encodes the definition of slope: the slope between any point (x, y) and the known point (x₁, y₁) equals m.
key_concept.context: Point-slope form is preferred when the slope and a point (not the y-intercept) are known. It is easily converted to slope-intercept form by distributing and solving for y. It is the 'universal' linear form—every non-vertical line can be written this way from any point on it.

image.alt: The point-slope formula prominently displayed: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). A diagram showing a line with known point (x₁, y₁) and a variable point (x, y). The slope formula (y−y₁)/(x−x₁) = m connects them, rearranged to y−y₁ = m(x−x₁).
image.caption: Point-slope form comes directly from the slope formula: (y − y₁)/(x − x₁) = m.

process_flow.process: Identify the Slope and a Point on the Line
process_flow.description: Determine the slope m and the coordinates of any point (x₁, y₁) that lies on the line.
process_flow.mechanism: The slope m can be given, calculated from two points, or determined from parallel/perpendicular relationships. The point (x₁, y₁) can be any point known to be on the line—the y-intercept, an x-intercept, a given point, or a point read from the graph.
process_flow.result: The slope m and coordinates (x₁, y₁) ready for substitution.
applications: Given two points: find m, then choose either point as (x₁, y₁).
Given a parallel line: same slope. Given a perpendicular line: negative reciprocal slope.

image.alt: A line with a point (2, −1) highlighted. A slope triangle shows m = 3. Labels: 'm = 3' and '(x₁, y₁) = (2, −1)'. The point-slope template y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) is shown ready for substitution.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the slope m and any point (x₁, y₁) on the line.

process_flow.description: Write the equation y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) by substituting the known values.
process_flow.mechanism: Replace m with the slope. Replace x₁ and y₁ with the coordinates of the known point. Be careful with signs: if y₁ is negative, y − (−3) becomes y + 3. If x₁ is negative, x − (−2) becomes x + 2. The form is most useful with the substitution clearly shown.
process_flow.result: The point-slope equation of the line.

image.alt: Substitution shown: m=3, x₁=2, y₁=−1 placed into y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). Result: y − (−1) = 3(x − 2) simplified to y + 1 = 3(x − 2). The point (2,−1) is on the graphed line.
image.caption: Step 2: Substitute m, x₁, and y₁ into y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). Watch the signs carefully.

process_flow.description: If needed, simplify the point-slope equation into slope-intercept form y = mx + b by distributing and solving for y.
process_flow.mechanism: Distribute m to both terms inside the parentheses. Then add or subtract to isolate y on one side. The result will be in the form y = mx + b, where the y-intercept b is revealed. This step is optional—point-slope form is a perfectly valid equation of the line.
process_flow.result: The slope-intercept form of the same line, equivalent to the point-slope form.
applications: Converting reveals the y-intercept without needing to find it separately.

image.alt: The algebraic conversion: y + 1 = 3(x − 2) → distribute: y + 1 = 3x − 6 → subtract 1: y = 3x − 7. Both forms displayed as equivalent. The graph shows the y-intercept at (0,−7).
image.caption: Step 3 (Optional): Convert to slope-intercept form by distributing and isolating y.

worked_examples.title: Writing Equations in Point-Slope and Slope-Intercept Forms
worked_examples.problem:
Write the equation of each line in both point-slope and slope-intercept form.

1) Slope = 4, passes through (3, 10).
2) Passes through (−1, 5) and (2, −4).
3) Parallel to y = 2x − 7 and passes through (4, 1).

step_by_step.step: 2) Given two points — find slope first

step_by_step.explanation:
Parallel to y = 2x − 7 → m = 2 (same slope).

Passes through (4, 1): y − 1 = 2(x − 4). (Point-slope)

y − 1 = 2x − 8 → y = 2x − 7. (Slope-intercept — interestingly, it's the same equation as the original line? Wait, (4,1) on y=2x−7: 2(4)−7=1 ✓. The point IS on the original line, so the 'parallel' line IS the original line.)

image.alt: Three graphs: (1) Line with m=4 through (3,10). (2) Line through (−1,5) and (2,−4) with m=−3. (3) Line parallel to y=2x−7 through (4,1) — same line. Each with point-slope and slope-intercept forms displayed.
image.caption: Writing linear equations: point-slope form from slope and a point, then conversion to slope-intercept.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Point-slope: y − 10 = 4(x − 3). Slope-intercept: y = 4x − 2.
2) Point-slope: y − 5 = −3(x + 1). Slope-intercept: y = −3x + 2.
3) Point-slope: y − 1 = 2(x − 4). Slope-intercept: y = 2x − 7.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Point-slope form from slope and a point, slope from two points, and parallel line slope relationships.

sections.section_title: Writing Equations of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
sections.introduction: The slope relationships for parallel and perpendicular lines make it straightforward to write equations for lines with these properties. Given a line and a point, you can write the equation of a parallel line (same slope) or a perpendicular line (negative reciprocal slope) through that point.

introduction_image.alt: A given line L with slope 3. Two lines through point P(2,1): a parallel line (also slope 3, equation y−1=3(x−2)) and a perpendicular line (slope −1/3, equation y−1=−1/3(x−2)). All three lines graphed with labels.
introduction_image.caption: Parallel line: same slope. Perpendicular line: negative reciprocal slope. Both through the same point.

key_concept.definition: To write the equation of a line parallel to a given line through a point (x₁, y₁): use the same slope as the given line. To write the equation of a line perpendicular to a given line through (x₁, y₁): use the negative reciprocal of the given line's slope. In both cases, apply point-slope form with the new slope and the given point.
key_concept.context: This is one of the most common applications of linear equations. It combines slope concepts from Section 3.6 with equation-writing skills to solve geometric problems algebraically.

image.alt: A reference card: Left — parallel line construction (keep m, use new point). Right — perpendicular line construction (use −1/m, use new point). Both with example: given y=2x+1 and point (3,4). Parallel: y−4=2(x−3). Perpendicular: y−4=−1/2(x−3).
image.caption: Parallel: same m. Perpendicular: negative reciprocal m. Then point-slope form.

worked_examples.title: Equations of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
worked_examples.problem:
1) Write the equation of the line parallel to y = 3x − 5 that passes through (2, 7).

2) Write the equation of the line perpendicular to y = −2x + 1 that passes through (−4, 3).

3) Given line L through (1, 2) and (5, 8), write the equation of the line perpendicular to L through (0, 0).

step_by_step.explanation:
Given line: y = 3x − 5 → m = 3.

Parallel line has same slope: m = 3.

Through (2, 7): y − 7 = 3(x − 2). (Point-slope)

Slope-intercept: y − 7 = 3x − 6 → y = 3x + 1.

Given line: y = −2x + 1 → m = −2.

Perpendicular slope = negative reciprocal: m⊥ = 1/2.

Through (−4, 3): y − 3 = (1/2)(x − (−4)) → y − 3 = (1/2)(x + 4).

Slope-intercept: y − 3 = (1/2)x + 2 → y = (1/2)x + 5.

step_by_step.step: 3) Perpendicular to line through two points
step_by_step.explanation:
Slope of L: m = (8−2)/(5−1) = 6/4 = 3/2.

Perpendicular slope: m⊥ = −2/3.

Through (0, 0): y − 0 = (−2/3)(x − 0) → y = −2/3 x.

image.alt: Three graphs: (1) Original line y=3x−5 and parallel line y=3x+1. (2) Original line y=−2x+1 and perpendicular line y=(1/2)x+5 with right angle marked. (3) Line L with m=3/2 and perpendicular line through origin y=−2/3 x.
image.caption: Writing equations of parallel (same slope) and perpendicular (negative reciprocal) lines.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Parallel lines (m₁ = m₂), perpendicular lines (m₁·m₂ = −1), point-slope form, and slope from two points.

sections.introduction: Mastery of linear equations requires practice writing equations in both forms, converting between forms, finding equations of parallel and perpendicular lines, and applying these skills in problem contexts.

worked_examples.title: Equations of Lines Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Write in slope-intercept form: slope = −5, y-intercept = 3.
2) Write in point-slope form: slope = 2/3, passes through (6, −1).
3) Write in both forms: passes through (4, 5) and (10, 17).
4) Write the equation parallel to y = −x + 4 through (−2, 6).
5) Write the equation perpendicular to y = 4x − 3 through (8, 1).

step_by_step.explanation:
Given m = −1. Parallel: m = −1.

Through (−2, 6): y − 6 = −1(x − (−2)) → y − 6 = −(x + 2).

Slope-intercept: y = −x + 4.

Given m = 4. Perpendicular: m = −1/4.

Through (8, 1): y − 1 = (−1/4)(x − 8).

Slope-intercept: y − 1 = −1/4 x + 2 → y = −1/4 x + 3.

image.alt: Five graphs corresponding to the five problems: (1) y=−5x+3. (2) Point-slope form through (6,−1). (3) y=2x−3 through two points. (4) Parallel line. (5) Perpendicular line with right angle marked.
image.caption: Equations of lines practice: slope-intercept, point-slope, two-point, parallel, and perpendicular.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) y = −5x + 3.
2) y + 1 = (2/3)(x − 6).
3) Point-slope: y − 5 = 2(x − 4). Slope-intercept: y = 2x − 3.
4) y = −x + 4.
5) y = −1/4 x + 3.
worked_examples.concept_applied: All linear equation forms, slope calculation, and parallel/perpendicular line construction.

summary: Linear equations can be written in multiple equivalent forms. Key points: 1) Slope-Intercept Form: y = mx + b. m is the slope, b is the y-intercept (where x = 0). Ideal for graphing and identifying key features. 2) Point-Slope Form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). Uses any point (x₁, y₁) and the slope m. Most flexible—works when the y-intercept is unknown. 3) Converting: Point-slope converts to slope-intercept by distributing m and isolating y. Both forms represent the same line. 4) Parallel Lines: Same slope (m₁ = m₂). To write a parallel line equation, use the same slope with the new point in point-slope form. 5) Perpendicular Lines: Negative reciprocal slopes (m₁·m₂ = −1). To write a perpendicular line equation, use −1/m with the new point. 6) Special Cases: Horizontal lines → y = constant (m = 0). Vertical lines → x = constant (undefined slope, cannot use y = mx + b). Through origin → y = mx (b = 0). 7) These forms bridge the geometric properties of lines (slope, intercepts, parallel/perpendicular) with their algebraic representations, enabling precise analysis and prediction.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Equations of Lines' in center. Branches: Slope-Intercept Form (y=mx+b, m=slope, b=y-intercept), Point-Slope Form (y−y₁=m(x−x₁), any point works), Converting Between Forms (distribute, isolate y), Parallel Lines (same m, use new point), Perpendicular Lines (negative reciprocal m, use new point), Special Cases (horizontal y=c, vertical x=c, through origin y=mx).
review_questions_image.caption: Equations of Lines Review: forms, conversions, and applications to parallel and perpendicular lines.
